Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Analytic Philosophy of Mind

Raimo Tuomela

1940 – 2022

Collective intentionality, we-mode agency, and the ontology of social minds.

Raimo Tuomela did not write a single systematic treatise called “the philosophy of mind.” His thought on mind has to be reconstructed from several intersecting projects: philosophy of psychology and action, scientific realism, materialist ontology, folk psychology, mental causation, collective intentionality, group agency, and social ontology. The result is not a standard “qualia-first” philosophy of mind, but a broad, action-theoretic and social philosophy of mind: minds are materially realized systems of intentional, practical, and socially structured capacities; they are not immaterial souls; they are causally involved in action; and they can operate not only in an individual “I-mode” but also in a collective “we-mode.” Tuomela’s 1977 Human Action and Its Explanation already presented a philosophical foundation for psychology, discussing mental events and states and defending a causal theory of action in which mental episodes are causally responsible for action.

The most compact formulation of Tuomela’s ontological background is found in the Finnish article “Onko maailma pelkkää ainetta?” — “Is the world mere matter?” — co-authored with Ari Peuhu in niin & näin. There Tuomela and Peuhu state the thesis that the world consists only of singular material objects, explicitly aligning this with nominalism and materialism and opposing immaterial entities such as a Cartesian mental substance or soul, an immaterial personal God in the Christian sense, and abstract entities such as propositions or properties. The article is crucial because it shows that the “soul” question is not a marginal distraction. It is named directly as a target of Tuomela’s materialist ontology.

1. The basic metaphysical stance: scientific-realist materialism without a soul-substance

Tuomela’s philosophy of mind begins from a thesis of ontological economy. In the Finnish article, the world is said to contain only singular material objects. This is not merely the claim that most things are material; it is a criterion for what may be counted as part of the world’s real furniture. Tuomela and Peuhu’s materialism is broad and a posteriori: what counts as material is not fixed by armchair physics, but by the best scientific theories used to explain the world. Their “scientia mensura” thesis says that, in describing the world, the criteria of existence are supplied by the scientific theories that best explain the world.

This is decisive for the soul. On Tuomela’s view, an immaterial soul in the Cartesian sense is excluded because it is neither spatiotemporal nor causally integrated into the scientific causal structure of the world. The same argument is directed at a causally efficacious but immaterial personal God, at least when God is conceived as a non-spatiotemporal person-like agent. Tuomela and Peuhu explicitly describe immaterial entities as not participating, actually or counterfactually, in causal interaction; spatiotemporality and causal participation become the marks by which ontology is disciplined.

This does not mean Tuomela is a crude “only current physics exists” reductionist. He and Peuhu also emphasize that truth is epistemic but objective, tied to a standpoint or background conceptual system. Their view is closer to internal realism than to old-style metaphysical realism: the world may be described through different conceptual systems, and the scientific method is treated as the best way to investigate causal structure, not as an already completed catalogue of reality. This matters because Tuomela’s materialism is not a simple dogma of present-day neuroscience. It is a regulative ontological stance: the mind is real only insofar as it can be integrated into the best causal-explanatory account of the material world.

2. Mental states are real, but materially realized

Tuomela’s materialism does not eliminate mind. The key move is the thesis that some objects in the world have intentional and other psychic states. Tuomela and Peuhu call this thesis O: certain worldly objects possess intentional and psychic states. O is compatible with materialism if intentional and psychic properties are understood materialistically, for example through supervenience: if two possible worlds differ intentionally, they must differ materially; if they are materially identical, they cannot differ intentionally.

This gives the basic mind-body picture. Mental states are not independent substances. They are states or properties of material beings. A person’s beliefs, intentions, memories, pains, and plans are not ghostly items attached to a body; they are materially grounded states of the person. Tuomela and Peuhu explicitly leave the more exact description of intentional states to the best explanatory theories of psychology and cognitive science. They also refuse to canonize folk psychology in advance: if the best psychology does not postulate some ordinary mental entity or category, then materialism need not preserve it as an item of ontology.

The strongest formulation is in their footnote on mental and social ontology. They say that “token physicalism” is generally accepted for mental states: mental entities are neurophysiological and real. The same note adds that social entities such as groups and states are not non-material objects; they supervene on individuals’ actions and mental states, especially joint actions, joint intentions, beliefs, and related attitudes. This is probably the most concise formulation of the extracted Tuomelian philosophy of mind: mental states are real, but their reality is not the reality of a separate soul-substance; it is the reality of materially grounded states of embodied agents.

3. Nonreductive or emergent materialism

Tuomela’s position is best read as nonreductive materialism, not eliminative materialism. The Finnish article explicitly notes that nonreductive or emergent materialism is defended in Marras and Tuomela’s 1994 work. The point is subtle but important. Tuomela can be a materialist while still allowing that psychological descriptions are not trivially replaceable by physics-language. Mental vocabulary may be indispensable for explaining action, reasoning, cooperation, responsibility, and social institutions.

This is also why his 1977 Human Action and Its Explanation is so important. The book is described as a systematic philosophical account of human action and explanation in the spirit of scientific realism; it discusses psychological concept formation and the nature of mental events and states, while defending a causal theory of action that refines the common-sense view that mental episodes are causally responsible for actions. Tuomela’s materialism therefore does not make beliefs, intentions, or reasons explanatorily idle. It insists that they must be understood within a scientifically respectable causal ontology.

This is reinforced by his later work on mental causation. His CV lists “A defense of mental causation” in Philosophical Studies and “The fate of folk psychology” in the volume Consciousness in Philosophy and Cognitive Neuroscience. A review of the consciousness volume summarizes Tuomela’s line as the view that folk psychology needs correction but should not be rejected wholesale through eliminative materialism. Taken together, the evidence points to a revisionary realism about the mental: ordinary mental concepts may need scientific correction, but the mental is not an illusion.

4. Consciousness and qualia: acknowledged, but not foundational

Tuomela is not primarily a theorist of phenomenal consciousness. His philosophy of mind is not organized around the “hard problem” in the Chalmersian sense. Still, he does address the pressure that qualia place on materialism. In “Onko maailma pelkkää ainetta?”, Tuomela and Peuhu note that intentional states do not exhaust psychic states, and they specifically mention the qualia problem — examples such as pain and smell — as an argument used against materialism. Their response is not a full phenomenological theory; they propose to treat the qualia problem as an epistemic problem rather than as an ontological refutation of materialism.

This is one of the limits of the system. Tuomela’s account tells us where consciousness must fit: within a material, causal, scientifically intelligible ontology. It also tells us what not to infer from consciousness: we should not infer an immaterial soul-substance. But it does not give a richly developed account of what subjective experience is like from the first-person point of view. His treatment of qualia is more defensive than constructive.

A French discussion of collective intentionality helps expose this gap. It notes that Tuomela’s later work speaks of “we-mode states” that individuals have as group members because of a group reason; the same French text argues that a full theory of collective intentionality should also account for the phenomenal dimension of “we” experience, not merely objective attributions of intentions to collectives. This suggests a useful supplement to Tuomela: his theory gives the structural, normative, and action-theoretic account of mind, but it leaves room for further phenomenology of individual and collective consciousness.

5. The soul: what Tuomela denies, and what he leaves open

Tuomela denies the soul in the strong metaphysical sense: no immaterial mental substance, no independent psychic bearer separable from the body, no Cartesian res cogitans. The Finnish materialism article explicitly names “mental substance or soul in Descartes’s sense” as one of the entities excluded by the thesis that only singular material objects exist. In this sense, Tuomela’s philosophy is anti-dualist and anti-spiritualist.

The death-related implication appears in Finnish reception of Tuomela’s essay “Tieteellinen maailmankäsitys ja kuoleman ongelma” — “The scientific world-conception and the problem of death.” Search-accessible excerpts from the essay state that the human “psyche” is, at bottom, the highly organized activity of a matter-energy system. A Finnish discussion of funerary and death interpretation summarizes Tuomela as treating death in light of scientific realism and scientia mensura, taking it to support denial of an immortal soul and an afterlife.

That said, Tuomela’s position does not force us to treat every use of “soul” language as meaningless. It forces a distinction. If “soul” means an immaterial substance that survives bodily death and causally interacts with the world, Tuomela rejects it. If “soul” is used poetically, ethically, liturgically, or as a name for the human person’s meaningful life, then it may belong to what Tuomela and Peuhu call the “conceptual order” rather than the “order of being.” They explicitly distinguish questions about real beings and causal relations from questions of meaning, epistemic justification, values, norms, and prescriptive matters. These latter domains do not require the postulation of non-material entities in ontology.

This is the key theological nuance. Tuomela is not saying that religious language cannot be socially meaningful, morally important, or existentially powerful. He is saying that religious language cannot add immaterial entities to the ontology of the world unless those entities can be admitted under the best causal-explanatory scientific theory. So a Tuomelian treatment of theology would divide theological claims into two classes: ontological claims about what exists, which are constrained by scientia mensura; and conceptual, normative, symbolic, or communal claims, which can be analyzed as meanings, values, norms, commitments, and social practices.

6. Theology is not a distraction: it clarifies the stakes of his materialism

The theological implications are especially sharp in relation to death and personal identity. The Finnish theological term bank notes that contemporary official church doctrines on the immortality of the soul are often restrained; it cites the Finnish Lutheran Catechism’s claim that at death the body decomposes and the soul “waits” for the day of resurrection, while also noting the theological problem that, without some continuity link between death and resurrection, it becomes difficult to say that the same person is resurrected. Tuomela’s materialism intensifies exactly this problem. If there is no immaterial soul-substance, then personal continuity after death cannot be secured by a soul “waiting” outside the body.

A theology compatible with Tuomela would have to take one of three paths. First, it could reinterpret soul-language as symbolic, moral, communal, or existential rather than ontological. Second, it could defend a materialist or resurrection-based account of personal continuity without an immaterial soul. Third, it could reject Tuomela’s scientia mensura criterion and defend a metaphysics in which divine and spiritual realities are not scientific posits within the causal order. The third option would be a direct philosophical disagreement, not a mere misunderstanding.

Finnish theological and religious-philosophical reception recognizes this conflict. A Finnish Christian-philosophical discussion classifies Tuomela’s scientia mensura as a strong form of scientism and argues that naturalism faces special problems with consciousness and mental states. Sami Pihlström’s work in Finnish philosophy of religion also notes that Tuomela’s scientia mensura-based argument for God’s non-existence appears in Tuomela’s 1983 Tiede, toiminta ja todellisuus. These sources are not neutral endorsements; they show that Finnish theological reception has understood Tuomela’s materialism as a serious metaphysical challenge to theism and immortality, not merely as a passing scientific attitude.

7. Mind as practical agency: beliefs, intentions, reasons, and action

Tuomela’s philosophy of mind is best approached through action, not through introspection. Mental states matter because they explain what agents do. In Human Action and Its Explanation, the central concerns include human action, mental episodes, psychological concept formation, causality, intention, and consciousness. His action theory is causalistic: intentions, beliefs, desires, acceptances, and other mental episodes are not inert descriptions; they are part of the causal explanation of human conduct.

This means that mind is not primarily an inner theater. It is a system of action-guiding states and capacities. Beliefs represent how things are; desires and wants orient agents toward possible outcomes; intentions settle practical direction; acceptances allow agents or groups to treat propositions as operative premises; commitments stabilize action over time. The mind is therefore purposive, practical, and explanatory.

This also helps explain why Tuomela is not an eliminativist. If one removed beliefs, intentions, and reasons from the explanatory vocabulary, one would also remove the conceptual machinery needed to explain action, cooperation, responsibility, and social institutions. His materialism revises mental vocabulary by scientific standards, but his action theory requires a robust explanatory role for the mental. His published work on “Belief versus Acceptance,” “Can collectives have beliefs?”, “Group beliefs,” “The fate of folk psychology,” and “A defense of mental causation” confirms that belief, acceptance, folk psychology, and mental causation were not peripheral topics for him.

8. The social expansion of mind: I-mode and we-mode

Tuomela’s most original contribution to a comprehensive philosophy of mind is his theory of collective intentionality. In The Philosophy of Sociality, he distinguishes the “I-mode” from the “we-mode.” The I-mode is the standpoint of an individual acting as a private person, even if the individual is acting in a group context. The we-mode is the standpoint of a person acting as a group member, from the group’s point of view, under a group reason. Oxford’s abstract of the chapter “The We-Mode and the I-Mode” says the we-mode involves a Collectivity Condition — the “standing and falling together” idea — and that its central ingredients are collective acceptance for the group and collective commitment.

This is a genuine philosophy of mind because it describes a mode of thinking, intending, believing, and reasoning. The we-mode is not merely several individuals having similar private intentions. It is a transformation of practical perspective. I do not merely ask, “What do I want?” or even “What do I want for the group?” I ask, “What are we to do, given our collectively accepted goal, norm, or ethos?” The group’s reason becomes my reason qua member.

A Notre Dame review captures the balance in Tuomela’s position: collective intentions cannot be reduced to individual intentions, but “at bottom” there remains nothing but individuals; the we-mode, like the I-mode, is a mode of reasoning, believing, and approaching the world possessed by individuals, not by collectives. This is the heart of Tuomela’s nonreductive individualism: collective intentionality is not reducible to private I-mode attitudes, yet there is no ghostly group subject over and above the members.

9. Collective acceptance, group beliefs, and religious communities

The notion of collective acceptance is especially important for theology. A religious community may collectively accept doctrines, creeds, liturgical meanings, moral obligations, sacred roles, and institutional authorities. Tuomela’s framework can analyze such phenomena without deciding that the accepted religious content is ontologically true. A group can accept a proposition “as true or correct for the group” through explicit or implicit agreement to share a we-mode joint attitude, as one review quotes Tuomela’s account of collective acceptance.

This gives a powerful way to understand theology sociologically and philosophically. A creed, for example, can function as a group belief or group acceptance. A church can act through officeholders under collectively accepted authority. A doctrine can structure the ethos of a community. Rituals can create and reinforce we-mode commitments. In this sense, Tuomela’s philosophy does not simply dismiss religion; it gives tools for analyzing religion as a social practice constituted by shared attitudes, norms, commitments, and institutions.

But this analysis is double-edged. It allows theological claims to be socially real without making their supernatural objects ontologically real. A doctrine may be real as a collectively accepted norm, but that does not mean the doctrine’s object exists in the order of being. Tuomela’s social ontology can explain how “God,” “soul,” “sin,” “grace,” or “resurrection” function within a community’s ethos, while his materialist ontology blocks those terms from referring to immaterial entities unless they pass the scientia mensura test.

10. Group minds: functional, extrinsic, not phenomenal

Tuomela’s theory naturally raises the question of group minds. Can a corporation, church, state, committee, or scientific community literally have a mind? Tuomela’s answer is carefully limited. He discusses “group agents and group minds” explicitly in the 2013 chapter “Who Is Afraid of Group Agents and Group Minds?” His later profile summary of “Group Agents and Their Responsibility” says that group agents are able to act but are not literally agents; some we-mode groups and corporations can be regarded as functional group agents, but they do not have intrinsic mental states or phenomenal features comparable to those of biological individual members.

So Tuomela permits group minds only in a functional and derivative sense. A group may have beliefs, intentions, commitments, and reasons when there are appropriate patterns of collective acceptance, authority, member action, and institutional structure. But a group does not have phenomenal consciousness. There is no “what it is like” to be a corporation, in the way there is something it is like to be an embodied human subject.

This is consistent with his materialism. The Finnish article’s footnote on social entities says groups and states are not non-material objects; they supervene on individuals’ actions and mental states. Thus, Tuomela’s “group mind” is not a collective soul. It is a normatively and functionally organized pattern of member-level mental states and actions.

There is a real philosophical cost here. A Notre Dame review notes that Tuomela’s collectives act through the we-mode actions of their members and that this may yield a weaker account of group action than some theorists want, especially for corporations or institutions that seem to act in ways no individual member fully controls. Still, the limitation is principled: Tuomela wants the explanatory benefits of group agency without postulating sui generis group minds.

11. Non-English reception: what it adds

The Finnish material is indispensable because it contains the clearest statements about materialism, the soul, God, death, and scientia mensura. The English-language reception often focuses on collective intentionality and social ontology; the Finnish sources reveal more directly how Tuomela’s scientific realism bears on immortality, the soul, and theological metaphysics.

The French reception adds a phenomenological pressure point. The EHESS volume on collective intentionality discusses Tuomela’s we-mode states and emphasizes that collective intentionality involves not only objective attribution of states to collectives, but also subjective and phenomenal dimensions of “we” experience. This is a useful corrective: Tuomela’s structure of group reasons and commitments may need supplementation by a richer account of lived first-person-plural consciousness.

The Spanish reception places Tuomela directly inside philosophy of mind. The journal Contrastes published “Intención conjunta y colectiva” by Tuomela in a supplement titled Filosofía actual de la mente — “Current Philosophy of Mind” — alongside essays on embodiment, computational theories of cognition, intersubjectivity, naturalism, subjective experience, neurobiology and consciousness, and social cognition. This confirms that Tuomela’s work on joint and collective intention is not merely political or sociological; it is part of the philosophy of mind because it concerns the structure of intentional states.

The Italian legal-ontological reception is also relevant. Corrado Roversi’s Italian discussion of collective intentionality and legal reality defines intentionality as the capacity of mental states to be directed toward objects and treats collective intentionality as the idea that some intentional states can be shared, thereby explaining aspects of social group behavior and institutions. This line of reception reinforces the idea that Tuomela’s social ontology is an extension of philosophy of mind into law, institutions, and norm-governed collective agency.

12. The extracted Tuomelian philosophy of mind: thirteen theses

A comprehensive philosophy of mind can be extracted from Tuomela as follows.

First, there is no immaterial mental substance. The soul in the Cartesian sense is rejected, and the human psyche must be understood as materially grounded. This is the anti-dualist core of the system.

Second, existence claims are governed by scientia mensura. What exists in the order of being is determined by the best explanatory scientific theories, especially insofar as they describe causal structure.

Third, mental states are real. Tuomela is not saying that beliefs, intentions, pains, and plans are fictional. He is saying that they are real only as states of material beings, ultimately anchored in neurophysiological reality.

Fourth, mental properties supervene on material properties. No intentional difference is possible without a material difference.

Fifth, the correct taxonomy of mental states is not fixed by common sense. Folk psychology may be corrected by psychology and cognitive science, though not necessarily eliminated wholesale.

Sixth, mental causation is legitimate. Tuomela’s action theory treats mental episodes as causally responsible for action, and his later work explicitly defends mental causation.

Seventh, consciousness does not refute materialism. Qualia are acknowledged, but Tuomela treats their difficulty as epistemic rather than as proof of ontological dualism.

Eighth, mind is practical before it is contemplative. The core mental categories are belief, intention, acceptance, reason, commitment, and action-guidance.

Ninth, the individual mind can operate in different modes. In the I-mode, the agent acts from an individual standpoint; in the we-mode, the agent acts as a group member under group reasons, collective acceptance, and collective commitment.

Tenth, collective intentionality is nonreducible in structure but individual-based in bearers. We-mode intentionality cannot be reduced to private I-mode states, yet it is still possessed by individuals, not by a mysterious collective subject.

Eleventh, group minds are functional and derivative. Groups may have beliefs and intentions in an extrinsic, institutionally organized, functionally grounded sense, but not intrinsic phenomenal consciousness.

Twelfth, social entities supervene on individual actions and mental states. A state, church, corporation, or scientific community is not an immaterial object; it is a structured social entity grounded in material persons and their intentional relations.

Thirteenth, theology is admissible as meaning, norm, social practice, and collective acceptance, but not as a source of immaterial ontology. Theological claims become philosophically acceptable to Tuomela when interpreted as conceptual, normative, existential, or social-practical claims; they become unacceptable when they posit causally efficacious immaterial beings in the order of being.

13. Strengths of the view

The first strength is integration. Tuomela connects philosophy of mind, philosophy of action, philosophy of psychology, social ontology, and philosophy of social science. Many theories of mind stop at the individual organism. Tuomela shows how the mind’s intentional structure extends into cooperation, institutions, social practices, authority, group responsibility, and collective knowledge. His Philosophy of Sociality is explicitly a systematic study of social groups, collective commitment, group intentions, beliefs, and actions.

The second strength is that he avoids both crude reductionism and metaphysical extravagance. He does not reduce collective intentionality to a mere sum of private attitudes, but he also refuses to posit a literal collective consciousness. This makes his account especially useful for institutions: it explains how groups can act, believe, accept, and commit without giving them souls.

The third strength is that the view takes theology seriously at the right level. It does not merely sneer at soul-language. It asks what ontological commitments soul-language carries. If the soul is an immaterial substance, Tuomela rejects it. If soul-language expresses a community’s self-understanding, values, practices, or existential orientation, his social ontology can analyze it as part of the conceptual and social order.

14. Weaknesses and unresolved problems

The first weakness is consciousness. Treating qualia as an epistemic problem may be too quick. It blocks an immediate dualist inference, but it does not explain why materially realized processes have subjective feel. A critic can accept Tuomela’s materialist ontology and still ask for a deeper account of phenomenal consciousness. The French discussion’s insistence that collective intentionality must address phenomenal “we” experience points to a broader gap: Tuomela has a powerful theory of intentional and practical structure, but not a comparably developed phenomenology.

The second weakness is scientia mensura itself. It is a philosophical principle, not a scientific result. Finnish reception has noticed this. One Finnish review of Tuomela’s Tiede, toiminta ja todellisuus raises the question whether, if scientia mensura is accepted, ontology can still say anything important when we do not yet know what future science will say. Sami Pihlström also notes that he has criticized Tuomela’s ontological commitments and his scientia mensura thesis in the scientific realism debate.

The third weakness is theology. Tuomela’s argument works best against Cartesian souls, spiritual substances, and a person-like immaterial God treated as an explanatory causal agent. It is less obviously decisive against classical theisms that do not construe God as one more entity inside the world’s causal network. A Thomist, for example, might say God is not a non-spatiotemporal object competing with scientific entities, but the metaphysical ground of being. Tuomela may still reject that move, but he would need more than scientia mensura alone to address it fully.

The fourth weakness is group agency. Tuomela’s groups may be too dependent on member action to capture large-scale institutional agency. The Notre Dame review notes that his account may leave no substantial sense in which collectives themselves author actions, since collectives act only through members acting as proxies. This is not a fatal objection, but it shows the cost of preserving materialist individual-bearer ontology while trying to do justice to group agency.

15. Final synthesis

Tuomela’s extracted philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence: mind is materially grounded, causally efficacious, action-guiding intentionality that can be individually or collectively structured, but it is never an immaterial soul and never a free-floating group consciousness.

His view of the soul is negative but not shallow: the soul as Cartesian or immortal substance is rejected; soul-language as meaning, value, identity, or social practice can be analyzed but not ontologically reified. His view of consciousness is materialist and epistemically cautious, though underdeveloped phenomenologically. His view of mind is strongest where mind becomes agency: believing, intending, accepting, committing, reasoning, acting, cooperating, and standing under shared group reasons.

The most distinctive contribution is the we-mode. Tuomela expands philosophy of mind beyond the isolated subject without abandoning materialism. There are no immaterial souls, and there is no literal group soul. But there are embodied persons whose minds can be reorganized by collective acceptance, collective commitment, group reasons, and shared ethos. That is the core of a Tuomelian philosophy of mind: a naturalistic theory of embodied intentional agency, extended into social and institutional life.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
